label: Lateral epicondylitis without fracture or neurovascular red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit lateral epicondylitis, often called tennis elbow.
found: Your clinician diagnosed uncomplicated lateral epicondylitis. We did not find fracture, tendon rupture, nerve or blood-flow problems, or an elbow emergency today.
home: Rest from activities that trigger sharp elbow pain.
Use ice, stretching, therapy exercises, or a brace only as your clinician recommended.
Return to gripping, lifting, and repetitive forearm activity gradually as symptoms improve.
meds: Use pain medicine only if prescribed or recommended, and follow the label.
Do not use medicine to push through worsening pain or weakness.
return: New numbness, weakness, blue or pale fingers, or trouble using the hand.
A pop, new deformity, inability to bend or straighten the elbow, or severe worsening pain.
Fever, a red hot swollen joint, new injury, or symptoms not improving as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, sports medicine, orthopedics, therapy, urgent care, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated lateral epicondylitis or tennis elbow.
No fracture/dislocation concern, tendon rupture, elbow instability, ulnar nerve symptoms, neurovascular compromise, infection, high-energy trauma, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Fracture or dislocation concern, tendon rupture, distal biceps or triceps concern, elbow instability, ulnar nerve symptoms, neurovascular compromise, septic joint or infection concern, open wound, high-energy trauma, or specialist-directed orthopedic plan.
must_not_miss: Distal biceps or triceps tendon rupture.
source_audit_notes: AAOS supports lateral epicondylitis as an overuse elbow condition, typical activity-related symptoms, nonsurgical care, and separate handling when fracture, nerve, neck, or other soft-tissue causes are suspected. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated lateral epicondylitis with fracture, rupture, ulnar nerve, infection, and neurovascular red flags absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of uncomplicated lateral epicondylitis or tennis elbow
no tendon rupture or ulnar nerve symptoms

label: Medial epicondylitis without fracture or neurovascular red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit medial epicondylitis, often called golfer's elbow.
found: Your clinician diagnosed uncomplicated medial epicondylitis. We did not find fracture, tendon rupture, elbow instability, nerve or blood-flow problems, or infection today.
home: Rest from activities that trigger sharp elbow pain.
Use ice, stretching, therapy exercises, or a brace only as your clinician recommended.
Return to gripping, throwing, lifting, and repetitive forearm activity gradually as symptoms improve.
meds: Use pain medicine only if prescribed or recommended, and follow the label.
Do not use medicine to push through worsening pain, numbness, or weakness.
return: New numbness or tingling in the ring or little finger, hand weakness, blue or pale fingers, or trouble using the hand.
A pop, new deformity, inability to bend or straighten the elbow, or severe worsening pain.
Fever, a red hot swollen joint, new injury, or symptoms not improving as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, sports medicine, orthopedics, therapy, urgent care, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated medial epicondylitis or golfer's elbow.
No fracture/dislocation concern, tendon rupture, elbow instability/UCL concern, ulnar nerve symptoms, neurovascular compromise, infection, high-energy trauma, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Fracture or dislocation concern, tendon rupture, distal biceps or triceps concern, elbow instability or UCL concern, ulnar nerve symptoms, neurovascular compromise, septic joint or infection concern, open wound, high-energy trauma, or specialist-directed orthopedic plan.
source_audit_notes: AAOS supports medial epicondylitis as an overuse elbow condition, nonsurgical care, and separation from UCL injury and ulnar nerve irritation. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated medial epicondylitis with fracture, instability, rupture, ulnar nerve, infection, and neurovascular red flags absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of uncomplicated medial epicondylitis or golfer elbow
no tendon rupture, instability, or ulnar nerve symptoms

label: Olecranon bursitis without septic joint or trauma red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit uncomplicated olecranon bursitis, swelling over the back of the elbow.
found: Your clinician diagnosed uncomplicated olecranon bursitis. We did not find septic joint, spreading infection, fracture, open wound, or another elbow emergency today.
home: Avoid leaning on or putting pressure on the elbow.
Use padding, rest, ice, or activity limits only as your clinician recommended.
Keep the skin over the elbow clean and protected.
meds: Use pain medicine only if prescribed or recommended, and follow the label.
Take antibiotics only if they were prescribed for you.
return: Fever, spreading redness, warmth, pus, red streaks, or feeling very ill.
New injury, open wound, severe pain, inability to move the elbow, numbness, or color change in the hand.
Swelling that rapidly worsens or does not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, sports medicine, orthopedics, urgent care, or the ED if swelling is not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated olecranon bursitis.
No septic joint or infection concern, open wound, fracture/dislocation concern, high-energy trauma, neurovascular compromise, immunocompromised host, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Septic joint, cellulitis, abscess, pus, fever, open wound, fracture or dislocation concern, high-energy trauma, neurovascular compromise, immunocompromised host, or specialist-directed orthopedic plan.
source_audit_notes: AAOS and MedlinePlus support olecranon bursitis as a distinct elbow-swelling pathway and support escalation for fever, redness, warmth, pain, pus, trauma, open wound, or infection concern. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated olecranon bursitis with septic joint, infection, fracture, wound, and neurovascular red flags absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of uncomplicated olecranon bursitis
no open wound or fracture concern

label: Forearm tendinitis without rupture or neurovascular red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit uncomplicated forearm tendinitis.
found: Your clinician diagnosed forearm tendinitis. We did not find tendon rupture, fracture, infection, nerve or blood-flow problems, or another emergency pathway today.
home: Rest from activities that make forearm pain worse.
Use ice, gentle motion, activity limits, or therapy exercises only as your clinician recommended.
Return to lifting, gripping, and repetitive work gradually as symptoms improve.
meds: Use pain medicine only if prescribed or recommended, and follow the label.
Do not use medicine to push through worsening pain or weakness.
return: New numbness, weakness, blue or pale fingers, or trouble using the hand.
A pop, new deformity, inability to bend or straighten the wrist or elbow, or severe worsening pain.
Fever, spreading redness, warmth, drainage, new injury, or symptoms not improving as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, sports medicine, orthopedics, therapy, urgent care, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated forearm tendinitis.
No tendon rupture, fracture/dislocation concern, hand tendon injury, infection, neurovascular compromise, high-energy trauma, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Tendon rupture, fracture or dislocation concern, hand tendon injury, open wound, infection concern, neurovascular compromise, high-energy trauma, inability to use the limb, or specialist-directed orthopedic plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports tendinitis framing, overuse-related tendon pain, activity modification, clinician-directed pain control, and separation from rupture, infection, trauma, or neurovascular compromise. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated forearm tendinitis with rupture, fracture, infection, hand tendon, and neurovascular red flags absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of uncomplicated forearm tendinitis

label: Thumb tendinitis without rupture or neurovascular red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit uncomplicated thumb tendinitis.
found: Your clinician diagnosed thumb tendinitis. We did not find tendon rupture, fracture, infection, nerve or blood-flow problems, or another hand emergency today.
home: Rest from activities that make thumb pain worse.
Use ice, splinting, gentle motion, or activity limits only as your clinician recommended.
Return to gripping, pinching, and repetitive thumb use gradually as symptoms improve.
meds: Use pain medicine only if prescribed or recommended, and follow the label.
Do not use medicine to push through worsening pain, numbness, or weakness.
return: New numbness, weakness, blue or pale fingers, or trouble moving the thumb.
A pop, new deformity, inability to bend or straighten the thumb, or severe worsening pain.
Fever, spreading redness, warmth, drainage, new injury, or symptoms not improving as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, hand clinic, sports medicine, orthopedics, therapy, urgent care, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated thumb tendinitis.
No tendon rupture, fracture/dislocation concern, open wound, infection, neurovascular compromise, high-energy trauma, or specialist-directed hand plan.
exclusion: Tendon rupture, hand tendon injury, fracture or dislocation concern, open wound, infection concern, neurovascular compromise, high-energy trauma, inability to use the limb, or specialist-directed orthopedic plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports tendinitis framing, overuse-related tendon pain, activity modification, clinician-directed pain control, and separation from rupture, infection, trauma, or neurovascular compromise. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated thumb tendinitis with rupture, fracture, wound, infection, and neurovascular red flags absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of uncomplicated thumb tendinitis
